results.title: Scheduling Analysis & Research with Compound | Compound AI posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content:
To view or add a comment, sign in

View profile for Masato Okuwaki

Building IrukaDark, a Desktop AI Assistant for Everyday PC Work | Founder & CEO at CORe Inc.

Launch: DexCode – AI Slide Creation Environment for Developers. Your AI Agent Builds the Deck. You Never Leave the Terminal. [...] You can now build slides while you code. Generate and edit decks in parallel: all directly from your CLI. I’m excited to announce that DexCode, the slide creation environment built specifically for developers, is now open-source!
100% Free: No accounts, no logins, no limits.
Terminal-First: Create and manage slides without ever leaving your command line.
Parallel Editing: Split sessions to edit multiple slides simultaneously.
AI Native: Built to work seamlessly with AI agents like Claude Code, Cursor, and Windsurf.
I personally "graduated" from PowerPoint because of DexCode. While tools like "Claude in PowerPoint" or Copilot are great, I hated the friction of constantly switching away from my terminal. [...] All my slides are HTML.
No PowerPoint. No Keynote. Every deck is generated HTML, rendered to PDF with headless Chromium.
Why? Because generic sales decks don't close deals. Every customer hears "we solve your problems" while looking at the same 14 slides everyone else sees. I wanted slides that actually hit THEIR problems. Their pain points from the discovery call. Their KPIs, their industry benchmarks. Their branding. Not a template with a swapped logo.
So I built a pipeline. My AI reads the meeting notes, extracts what matters, and generates a deck around it. CSS variables handle the colors. The content is the hard part, and that's where the AI comes in again. It builds slides that reference their exact challenges and connects them to our value proposition, not generic feature lists.

results.title: FlowDeck, Apple Development CLI for AI Agents & Claude Code
results.content:
The problem

## The Apple build loop is broken for agents.

xcodebuild was built for CI and scripts. It is not what Xcode uses, and not something an agent can drive and read on its own.

Five separate tools to list, boot, build, install, and launch

Walls of xcodebuild output no agent can parse

Hand-rolled scripts that break on every Xcode update

Your agent flying blind, with no idea what actually ran

The FlowDeck way: one command

~/Developer/WeatherApp · flowdeck

```
$ flowdeck -S "iPhone 16" ✓ Build Completed ✓ ✓ ✓ App Launched Successfully --jsonfor structured, CI-ready output
```

See the full before / after comparison →

The CLI

## Never open Xcode again. [...] > Donny Wals

One unified tool Replaces Apple's fragmented CLIs

Real-time log streaming Agents see what breaks instantly

results.title: Why One AI Agent Is Never Enough
results.content: G to generate a config for this project. The deck pops up a confirmation asking whether you want to instruct your agent to analyze the project and create a new one. Just select yes, and then hit the enter key. Now, what happens next is that the deck sends a long structured prompt to the agent which just launched. It tells the agent to look at the code base, understand what kind of project it is, figure out which roles make sense, and produce a config file with sensible defaults. The agent does the work. We just wait. Once the agent reports that it's done, let's take a look at what it produced. We can do that with without leaving the deck, right? The exclamation mark prefix in the prompt tells the agent to run a small command on our behalf, So, we I'm going to ask it to print out the [...] to use Vim or similar tools. And then, you type whatever command you want to use to launch your agent in the command field. That could be Claude or open code or anything else, right? Depending on which agent you prefer. In my case, I'm using devbox run agent, which launches Claude Code inside a devbox shell. I'm freaky. I know that most of you don't use it, but that's fine. Now, what you should see at this point is the new agent dialog with the project directory filled in, a default name derived from that directory, and the command field ready for your input. With the deck created, we can move on to generating the orchestration config itself. To trigger the generation, just press Ctrl D to enter the command mode, and press G to generate a config for this project. The deck pops up a [...] The coder is the agent that actually writes the code. When orchestrator hands off a task, the coder opens files, modifies them, writes the tests, runs the tests, and reports back when it's done. It's the most labor-intensive role in the pipeline, which is why you typically point your most at it. Implementation is the hardest work in the chain, and shortcuts here show up downstream as broken builds or bad reviews. Then we have the reviewer. Once the coder reports done, the reviewer takes a look at the change. Its job is to find problems, bugs, missed edge cases, sloppy patterns, everything everything that should not ship. The reviewer runs independently of the coder with its own fresh context, its own instructions. That alone removes most of the bias because the reviewer doesn't carry

results.title: I Didn’t Write a Single Line of Code. The System Did. | by Satyajit Patra | Apr, 2026 | Towards AI
results.content:

## Towards AI

·

Towards AI

We build Enterprise AI. We teach what we learn. Join 100K+ AI practitioners on Towards AI Academy. Free: 6-day Agentic AI Engineering Email Guide: 

# I Didn’t Write a Single Line of Code. The System Did.

## The rise of Harness Engineering — and why your AI agent setup is probably broken.

Satyajit Patra

Satyajit Patra

7 min read

·

Apr 30, 2026

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI-driven strategy execution interface

If you’ve been building with AI agents in 2025–2026, you’ve probably had this experience: You paste a Jira ticket into your AI coding tool. You tell it “implement this.” And then you wait. [...] ## 3. We Found Out Where Things Actually Break

Here’s the stat that should keep you up at night: 65% of enterprise AI failures trace back to harness defects — specifically context drift, schema misalignment, and state degradation. Not reasoning failures. Not model limitations “The harness”.

## The Five Pillars of a Harness

Every agent harness, whether simple or complex, is built from these same five components:

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Anatomy of an AI harness

## 1. Tool Orchestration

An agent’s capability is defined by the tools it can access. Tool orchestration means defining which tools are available, how they’re invoked, and what permissions they require. File system access, shell commands, API calls, database queries, external service integrations. [...] Press enter or click to view image in full size

The agent? That’s the application running on top. It only works if the OS underneath is solid. Nobody ships software directly on bare metal without an OS. But that’s exactly what most people do with AI agents — throw a prompt at a model and hope for the best.

You wouldn’t run software directly on a CPU without an operating system. You need something to manage memory, schedule processes, handle I/O.

Same logic applies here. You don’t deploy an AI agent without a harness to manage context, coordinate tools, and handle failures.

results.title: How AI transformed my data analysis workflow | Brian Julius posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content: 🤖 Your AI Agent Just Asked a Question. But Your Data is From Yesterday. Here’s the uncomfortable truth about agentic AI in 2025: ⚙️ Batch processing is killing your AI agents. Think about it. You’ve built this sophisticated AI agent — it can reason, plan, and execute complex workflows. But when it needs to make a decision, you’re feeding it stale data from last night’s ETL job. It’s like asking a Formula 1 driver to race while looking in the rearview mirror. 🏎️💨 🤯 The Agentic AI Paradox 🔴 Agents need to ACT in real-time 🔴 But they’re THINKING with yesterday’s data 🔴 Result? 🤖 Hallucinations, ❌ Wrong decisions, 💔 Broken trust The latency between “what’s happening” and “what the agent knows” isn’t just a technical inconvenience — it’s a fundamental bottleneck that breaks the entire
